title: What happens to Ohio auto suppliers if tariffs on Mexican components rise by 15%?
headline: Midwest auto suppliers face immediate component cost pressure.
summary: A 15% tariff increase on selected Mexican auto components raises first-order input pressure for U.S. assemblers and tier suppliers, with strongest sensitivity in powertrain and wire-harness categories.
caveats: Supplier contracts may delay pass-through timing.
Some components have short-term qualification constraints.

title: What happens to Michigan auto electronics if Mexican subassembly tariffs move +10%?
headline: Moderate substitution pressure in auto-electronics supply.
summary: Exposure suggests manageable but notable margin pressure as firms evaluate backup sourcing and inventory buffering.
caveats: Inventory strategy heavily influences near-term outcomes.

title: What happens to California electronics importers if tariffs on Chinese boards rise by 20%?
headline: Strong cost and substitution pressure for PCB-heavy buyers.
summary: Electronics importers face high first-order exposure in printed circuit boards and display modules, with sourcing shifts likely toward Vietnam and South Korea.
caveats: Alternative supplier scale can vary by product complexity.
Lead times may rise during transition periods.

title: What happens to Texas electronics assemblers if China connector tariffs rise by 12%?
headline: Connector and module costs tighten margins in assembly operations.
summary: Medium-high exposure in connector and module inputs points to tactical dual sourcing and renegotiation pressure.
caveats: Contract terms may temporarily absorb cost shifts.

title: What happens to Pennsylvania metals processors if tariffs on Canadian rolled steel rise by 8%?
headline: Moderate feedstock pressure with manageable substitution paths.
summary: Pennsylvania processors see first-order cost pressure in rolled steel inputs but retain partial flexibility through grade substitution and alternate contracts.
caveats: Metallurgical grade requirements limit substitution in specialty applications.

title: What happens to U.S. exporters if Canadian metals retaliation risk rises under a 10% tariff move?
headline: Export retaliation risk increases for bilateral metals trade.
summary: Retaliatory exposure raises first-order demand softness risk for select U.S. metals exporters.
caveats: Retaliation timing and scope are policy-dependent.

title: What happens to U.S. machinery buyers if tariffs on German motors increase by 12%?
headline: Machinery buyers face medium first-order input pressure.
summary: High-spec motor and bearing categories absorb most pressure, encouraging phased dual-source strategies.

title: What happens to Midwest machinery exporters if German retaliation risk increases with a 9% tariff shift?
headline: Export-side softness risk rises for machinery niches.
summary: First-order demand uncertainty increases in selected machinery export channels.
caveats: Broader demand cycles may dominate policy effects.

title: What happens to Gulf Coast chemical buyers if tariffs on Indian intermediates rise by 14%?
headline: Intermediate chemical input pressure increases across Gulf Coast operations.
summary: High exposure in precursor compounds suggests margin pressure and selective feedstock re-optimization.
caveats: Quality and compliance standards vary by molecule class.

title: What happens to California electronics firms if Vietnamese inputs face a 7% tariff increase?
headline: Low-to-moderate pressure with selective reshuffling of assembly nodes.
summary: Exposure is lower than China-linked scenarios but still relevant in connector and PCB categories.
caveats: Indirect exposure through upstream networks is not fully modeled.

title: What happens to U.S. med-tech importers if Korean device component tariffs increase by 11%?
headline: Moderate cost pressure in qualified medical component lanes.
summary: First-order pressure appears in sensor and polymer categories, with substitution constrained by regulatory qualification pathways.
caveats: Validation timelines can materially delay supplier shifts.

title: What happens to U.S. medical exporters if Korean retaliation risk rises under a 13% policy shift?
headline: Export retaliation risk may pressure selected med-tech demand.
summary: Exposure suggests potential demand softness in regulated device channels under reciprocal measures.
caveats: Healthcare procurement cycles can dampen immediate effects.
